elements.sent-2411-001: This study proposes a novel approach for real-time facial expression recognition utilizing short-range Frequency-Modulated Continuous-Wave (FMCW) radar equipped with one transmit (Tx), and three receive (Rx) antennas.
elements.sent-2411-002: The system leverages four distinct modalities simultaneously: Range-Doppler images (RDIs), micro range-Doppler Images (micro-RDIs), range azimuth images (RAIs), and range elevation images (REIs).
elements.sent-2411-003: Our innovative architecture integrates feature extractor blocks, intermediate feature extractor blocks, and a ResNet block to accurately classify facial expressions into smile, anger, neutral, and no-face classes.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our model achieves an average classification accuracy of 98.91\% on the dataset collected using a 60 GHz short-range FMCW radar.
elements.sent-2411-005: The proposed solution operates in real-time in a person-independent manner, which shows the potential use of low-cost FMCW radars for effective facial expression recognition in various applications.
elements.sent-2411-006: Facial Expression Recognition (FER) is a crucial technique for identifying emotional states in humans by analyzing their facial expressions \cite{SurveyFER .}
elements.sent-2411-007: By accurately detecting and analyzing these expressions, FER finds practical applications in enhancing Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) systems\cite{UFace , virtual reality experiences \cite{vrFER , and digital entertainment \cite{IntroFER,digitalEntFER .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Despite the prevalence of vision-based approaches in FER \cite{visionFER,visionFER2,visionFER3 , they are hindered by factors such as lighting and occlusion.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Short-range radars have gained popularity in both academia and industry due to their robustness in various environmental conditions and their privacy advantages.
elements.sent-2411-011: They are primarily used for indoor applications, including human presence detection, human activity classification, people counting, gesture recognition, and out-of-distribution (OOD) detection \cite{human_presence7,kahya2023hood,human_activity3,kahya2023harood,gesture_recog,RB-OOD,MCROOD .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Additionally, they are employed for vital sign monitoring, such as heartbeat estimation \cite{heartbeat_est , and for capturing facial features in authentication systems \cite{dnn-based,cnn-based,one-shot,point-cloud-face,kahya2024food .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: They are also used in facial expression recognition systems.
elements.sent-2411-014: The system employs a novel dual-locating approach to accurately position subjects' faces in space and extract facial muscle movements from noisy raw signals.
elements.sent-2411-015: While FER offers a fundamental method for extracting emotional states from facial expressions, some radar-based studies utilizing vital signs for emotion recognition provide additional insights into the complex interplay of physiological responses.
elements.sent-2411-016: Continuous-wave (CW) Doppler radar, for instance, has been instrumental in classifying emotions by capturing respiratory signal features from video stimuli \cite{emot_recog1,emot_recog2 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Similarly, millimeter-wave radar can monitor heartbeats and/or respiration, extracting features that empower deep/machine learning models in recognizing emotions\cite{emot_recog3,emot_recog6,mmWaveEmot_Recog .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, a non-contact multimodal system leverages a CW Doppler radar and a camera to capture respiratory and heartbeat signals for enhanced emotion recognition\cite{emot_recog4 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In our work, we introduce FERT, a novel facial expression recognition system using short-range FMCW radar.
elements.sent-2411-020: This system is designed to accurately classify smile, anger, neutral, and no-face expressions in a person-independent and real-time manner.
elements.sent-2411-021: Unlike camera-based solutions, our radar-based solution aims to model the specific movements of the muscles on the face while performing the specific expression.
elements.sent-2411-022: Therefore, in its unique pipeline, FERT benefits from Range-Doppler Images (RDIs), micro-RDIs, Range Azimuth Images (RAIs), and Range Elevation Images (REIs) simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-023: The system includes dedicated feature extractor blocks for each modality, ensuring comprehensive feature extraction from all four data types.
elements.sent-2411-024: After initial feature extraction, the intermediate features of similar modalities are combined: RDIs are merged with micro-RDIs, and RAIs with REIs.
elements.sent-2411-025: The combined intermediate features are fed into two separate intermediate feature extractors—one for RDIs \& micro-RDIs, and another for RAIs \& REIs.
elements.sent-2411-026: Subsequently, the outputs from these intermediate feature extractors are further combined to integrate all modalities at a certain point.
elements.sent-2411-027: The unified features are then fed into a ResNet block for the classification task.
elements.sent-2411-028: This sophisticated approach allows FERT to achieve high accuracy and real-time performance, making it a robust solution for facial expression recognition.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, they are employed for vital sign monitoring, such as heartbeat estimation \cite{heartbeat_est , and for capturing facial features in authentication systems \cite{dnn-based,cnn-based,one-shot,point-cloud-face .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: They are also utilized in facial expression recognition systems.
elements.sent-2411-031: Motivated by the potential of short-range radars as an alternative for emotion recognition using facial expressions, we introduce FERT, a novel facial expression recognition system designed to accurately classify smile, anger, neutral, and no-face expressions in a person-independent and real-time manner.
elements.sent-2411-032: This sophisticated approach allows FERT to achieve high accuracy and real-time performance, making it a robust solution for facial expression recognition.\fi \cite{mm3DFace employs 3D range angle spectrum (range-azimuth-elevation) of mmWave radar with three transmit (Tx) and four receive (Rx) antennas for 3D facial reconstruction, capturing changes in facial expressions using triplet loss embedding.}
elements.sent-2411-033: While FER offers a fundamental method for extracting emotional states from facial expressions, studies utilizing vital signs for emotion recognition provide additional insights into the complex interplay of physiological responses.
elements.sent-2411-034: Additionally, a non-contact multimodal system leverages CW Doppler radar and cameras to capture respiratory and heartbeat signals for enhanced emotion recognition\cite{emot_recog4 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: This sophisticated approach allows FERT to achieve high accuracy and real-time performance, making it a robust solution for facial expression recognition.\fi.
elements.sent-2411-036: This study utilizes Infineon's BGT60TR13C $\SI{60 {\giga\hertz}$ FMCW radar chipset, which has one Tx antenna and three Rx antennas.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The radar configuration is detailed in Table \ref{tab:radar_conf .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The Tx antenna emits \(N_c\) chirp signals, which are reflected and captured by the Rx antennas.
elements.sent-2411-039: These signals are mixed and low-pass filtered to derive the intermediate frequency (IF) signal.
elements.sent-2411-040: The IF signal is then digitized to produce the raw Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) data, organized as \(N_{Rx \times N_c \times N_s\), where \(N_c\) represents slow time and \(N_s\) fast time samples.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We generate macro and micro-RDIs, RAIs, and REIs as input to our model.
elements.sent-2411-042: Macro RDI is produced by first applying Range-FFT to the fast-time signal for range extraction, followed by mean removal and moving target identification (MTI) to eliminate static targets.
elements.sent-2411-043: Doppler-FFT then processes the slow-time signal to capture phase variations, producing the macro RDI.
elements.sent-2411-044: Micro-RDI is generated in a similar manner using Range-FFT on range data.
elements.sent-2411-045: Noise reduction is achieved by stacking eight range spectrograms and removing means from both the fast and slow time signals.
elements.sent-2411-046: Enhanced target detection is facilitated through Sinc filtering, and a final Doppler-FFT along the slow-time dimension completes the micro-RDI generation.
elements.sent-2411-047: For RAI and REI generation, we use the micro-RDI and apply the Direction of Arrival (DoA) estimation with the Capon beamforming algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-048: RAI and REI are obtained using two antennas, given our chipset's L-shaped configuration in the Rx antennas.
elements.sent-2411-049: The two antennas on one side of the L are used for RAI, while the other two antennas on the other side of the L are used for REI.
elements.sent-2411-050: As a final preprocessing step, we applied E-RESPD \cite{kahya2023hood to enhance the capture of facial movements on both RDIs, RAI and REI.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{ FMCW Radar Configuration Parameters } \begin{tabular}{@ {\extracolsep{10pt}} ccc} Configuration name Symbol Value Number of transmit antennas $N\textsubscript{Tx}$ 1 Number of receive antennas $N\textsubscript{Rx}$ 3 Chirps per frame $N\textsubscript{c}$ 64 Samples per chirp $N\textsubscript{s}$ 128 Frame period T\textsubscript{f} 50 \si{\ms} Chirp to chirp time $T\textsubscript{cc}$ 391.55 \si{\us} Bandwidth $B$ $\SI{1}{\giga\hertz}$ \end{tabular} \label{tab:radar_conf}
elements.figure-2411-002: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Figures/fert-pipeline-latest.pdf}} \caption{ This figure shows the overall architecture of FERT. The section with the yellow background consists of four feature extractor blocks for RDI, micro-RDI, RAI, and REI inputs, respectively. The section with the green background contains two intermediate feature extractor blocks for merged modalities: RDI \& micro-RDI and RAI \& REI. The section with the blue background is the ResNet block, which integrates all modalities for the final classification. All blocks are trained simultaneously to achieve highly accurate facial expression recognition.} \label{fig:pipline}
elements.sent-2411-051: Recognizing facial expressions is crucial for understanding a person's emotional state, which is essential for smart applications to deliver appropriate content.
elements.sent-2411-052: For instance, a driver's emotional state helps a smart car suggest the next action, while a user's emotional state influences the content provided by a smart glass.
elements.sent-2411-053: Therefore, FERT proposes a real-time, robust facial expression recognition pipeline using low-cost, easily integrable FMCW radar in a privacy-preserving manner.
elements.sent-2411-054: FERT consists of seven distinct blocks: four feature extractor blocks for RDI, micro-RDI, RAI, and REI modalities; two intermediate feature extractor blocks for paired modalities; and a ResNet34 \cite{resnet block that takes input from all modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The feature extractors share the same architecture, each consisting of three consecutive convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-056: Each layer includes 2D convolution, 2D batch normalization, and ReLU activation.
elements.sent-2411-057: The first layer uses 8 filters, while the second and third layers each use 16 filters, all with a $3 \times 3$ kernel size.
elements.sent-2411-058: These feature extractor blocks are trained only with their respective modalities to learn their low-level features.
elements.sent-2411-059: After these blocks, similar modalities are merged: RDIs with micro-RDIs, and RAIs with REIs.
elements.sent-2411-060: The two intermediate feature extractor blocks handle the merged modalities, consisting of two consecutive convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-061: Each layer sequentially applies 2D convolution, 2D batch normalization, ReLU activation, and 2D max pooling, using 32 filters with a $3 \times 3$ kernel size.
elements.sent-2411-062: These blocks learn the intermediate features of the merged modalities.
elements.sent-2411-063: The outputs of these blocks are then merged to integrate all modalities.
elements.sent-2411-064: The ResNet block takes this merged output, which includes information from all modalities, as input.
elements.sent-2411-065: It learns from all modalities and performs the final classification.
elements.sent-2411-066: For a comprehensive view of the entire pipeline, please refer to Figure \ref{fig:pipline .}
elements.sent-2411-067: The entire network is trained simultaneously using cross-entropy loss and an SGD optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-068: Our architecture is a fast learner, so it is trained only three epochs to reach the final results.
elements.sent-2411-069: Our experiments are conducted on a system equipped with an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 GPU, an Intel Core i7-11800H CPU, and 32GB of DDR4 RAM.
elements.sent-2411-070: In this research, we collected a facial expression dataset using Infineon's BGT60TR13C $\SI{60 {\giga\hertz}$ FMCW radar chipset over one month.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The radar sensor was positioned 25 cm away to capture the individual's facial expressions, with participants placing their chins on a table for alignment.
elements.sent-2411-072: Each recording session lasted between 1 to 5 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-073: We captured three primary facial expressions—anger, smile, and neutral—and one no-face class.
elements.sent-2411-074: The anger expression involved the person clenching their teeth, furrowing their brow, and narrowing their eyes.
elements.sent-2411-075: The smile was captured by having the person smile broadly, showing their teeth, and raising their cheeks.
elements.sent-2411-076: The neutral expression was maintained without any specific facial movement.
elements.sent-2411-077: We repeated the same movements for the corresponding facial expressions during each recording session.
elements.sent-2411-078: Importantly, individuals did not wear any accessories on their faces, such as glasses, during the recordings.
elements.sent-2411-079: The data was collected in various rooms to introduce environmental variability.
elements.sent-2411-080: Our dataset consists of four classes: anger, smile, neutral facial expressions, and no face in front of the radar.
elements.sent-2411-081: The training dataset includes 240330 data frames, while the testing dataset contains 105569 data frames, both sourced from two individuals in a balanced way for each expression.
elements.sent-2411-082: For real-time experiments, we also tested on individuals who did not exist in either training or test sets.
elements.sent-2411-083: Written consent has been obtained from all participants involved in the research.
elements.sent-2411-084: In our experiments, we always use completely different recordings for training and testing.
elements.sent-2411-085: To avoid overfitting to specific locations where the radar is positioned, we use distinct locations for experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-086: Our solution achieves highly accurate classification results of 98.85\%, 98.83\%, 97.92\%, and 100.0\% for smile, anger, neutral, and no-face classes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-087: To demonstrate the network's generalization capability, we also test our pipeline in real-time with participants not involved in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-088: This ensures that FERT performs accurately regardless of location or participant variability.
elements.sent-2411-089: Additionally, we provide a confusion matrix in Figure \ref{fig:conf_matrix_FERT for facial expression classification to present a detailed analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Our real-time demo videos are available here\footnote{\href{https://youtu.be/N2z488zxauQ {https://youtu.be/N2z488zxauQ .}}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.8\columnwidth]{Figures/conf-matrix-fert.pdf} \caption{ Confusion matrix to demonstrate the classification performance of FERT.} \label{fig:conf_matrix_FERT}
elements.sent-2411-091: In our pre-processing step, inspired by \cite{kahya2023hood , we use E-RESPD approach.}
elements.sent-2411-092: E-RESPD is a simple yet effective approach that uses consecutive 200 frames simultaneously instead of performing framewise usage.
elements.sent-2411-093: The idea also positively affects the overall classification performance of our pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-094: Therefore, we perform an ablation study to demonstrate the impact of the E-RESPD approach.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{ Ablation study for E-RESPD. } \begin{tabular}{@{\extracolsep{\fill}}ccccc} {} {} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Accuracy } SMILE ANGER NEUTRAL NO FACE FERT w/o E-RESPD 80.97 87.75 75.65 99.97 FERT 98.85 98.83 97.92 100.0 \end{tabular} \label{classification-res}
elements.sent-2411-096: In conclusion, this study presents a novel real-time facial expression recognition method using a low-resolution FMCW radar system.
elements.sent-2411-097: By employing RDI, micro-RDI, RAI, and REI modalities, our approach effectively captures the necessary features for distinguishing between different facial expressions.
elements.sent-2411-098: Our architecture's feature extractor, intermediate feature extractor, and ResNet blocks enable robust classification performance, achieving a 98.91\% average accuracy on the collected dataset.
elements.sent-2411-099: Our system's real-time capability and high accuracy underscore the viability of using low-cost and privacy-preserving radar technology for facial expression recognition in areas such as human-computer interaction, virtual reality, and digital entertainment.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
